text: The surgeon opens the gastrocolic omentum, revealing the tattoo marking the location of the tumor. The tumor appears smaller following neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and the surgeon assesses its proximity to the stomach to determine if a partial gastrectomy is necessary.

The surgeon encounters significant edema around the tumor, which is adhered to the greater curvature of the stomach. An en bloc resection of the stomach is performed, creating a window beneath the stomach for a wedge gastrectomy. The mesentery is opened along the greater curve near the fundus using a reinforced green load stapler.

The surgeon applies the final stapler to complete the gastrectomy. The staple line shows good perfusion, although there is some bleeding observed. The resected stomach is visible, indicating the completion of this part of the surgery.

The surgeon uses the final load of the stapler to achieve retraction, facilitating the mobilization of the descending colon with lateral to medial mobilization. The greater omentum is retracted upwards, exposing the colon. The left branch of the middle colic artery is taken down, revealing a dilated colon proximal to the tumor.

The surgeon lines up the bowel in an isoperistaltic fashion, performing a side-to-side anastomosis. Sutures are placed for retraction without tying, and the stapler is positioned along the suprapubic area. The distal suture is removed and repositioned proximally to hold the common enterotomy.

The surgeon places a suture on the proximal part of the common colotomy for retraction, tying it to secure the area. Due to the larger opening in the proximal colon, the suture is repositioned to the bottom of the colon crotch. Both proximal and distal silk sutures are used to gain momentum and cover the area before proceeding to close the common colotomy.

The surgeon uses canal stitches to invert the bowel, starting with regular bites before switching to canal stitches. The surgical field shows the resected stomach, and the pathology results indicate that the tumor was close to but not invading the stomach wall, with thirty lymph nodes tested negative.
The video shows the stomach and the pancreas in the surgical field.
